document_name: HAAD Coding Manual + Clinical Coding Process Review
version: HAAD Coding Manual V12 + Clinical Coding Process Review
rank_note: HAAD governs local Abu Dhabi coding processes, facility policies, coder requirements, documentation policies, and physician query processes.
scope: Abu Dhabi coding, documentation, outpatient visit coding, and clinical coding process rules

document_1.official_title: Health Authority Abu Dhabi Coding Manual V12

haad.scope: Local Abu Dhabi healthcare coding and compliance requirements
haad.precedence: Use first for Abu Dhabi local coding/process rules

ama_2021.precedence: Use after HAAD when the question is about office/outpatient E/M code selection

cms_1997.precedence: Use as fallback when HAAD/AMA do not answer the question

standards: Apply accurate, complete, and consistent coding practices.
Report all healthcare data elements completely and accurately.
Assign and report only codes that are clearly supported by health record documentation.
Query the provider when documentation is conflicting, incomplete, or ambiguous.
Refuse to change codes or narratives in a way that misrepresents meaning.
Refuse coding practices intended to inappropriately increase payment or skew data.
Protect the confidentiality of the health record.
Demonstrate integrity and commitment to ethical and legal coding practices.

rules: Coders must be aware of the references available for coding.
Coders must have access to HAAD Coding Manual, HAAD Adjudication rules, and other standards information from Shafafiya.
Additional references such as CMS, AMA, AHA, and AHIMA may be used as required.
The facility must use one guideline set consistently; mixing guideline sets is not acceptable.
standard_references_policy.guideline_mix_rule: A facility must state one guideline or another; a combination of guideline sets is not acceptable.

requirements: Each coder and staff involved with clinical information should understand HAAD Code of Ethics.
A coder-physician query process must exist for unclear or insufficient clinical documentation, with timelines.
Coders should have the patient's medical record available when requesting pre-authorization or pre-certification.
Coding quality checks and coding-related resubmissions must be governed by policy.

rules: Completion of documentation should occur within 24 hours of the service being rendered.
The medical record must be complete.
Documentation must support medical necessity for rendered services and evaluated conditions.
The reason for the encounter, relevant history, physical examination findings, and proper diagnostic test results must be documented.
Assessment, clinical impression or diagnosis, and medical plan of care must be documented.
Corrections, delayed entries, amendments, authentications, time, date, and signatures must be handled under record-keeping policy.
PHI security and privacy must be protected.
EHR documentation integrity must be maintained.
Copy-paste misuse in EHR documentation should be controlled by policy.
Narrative diagnosis is preferred over dropdown-only selection.
New staff should be oriented to the hospital/center, department, responsibilities, and technical applications.
Staff should receive ongoing training and education to maintain or advance skills and meet credential requirements.
All personnel must be knowledgeable about their roles in the organization.
Training should be relevant to the settings in which the staff member works.
Coding staff should review HAAD coding guidelines, specialty topics, E/M documentation guidelines, ICD-10-CM awareness, and coding updates.

coder_query_process.source_section: Coding Practice Policies + Clinical Coding Process Review

rules: Receive or retrieve the medical record from the clinic.
Review the clinic note for diagnoses and procedures, or the reason for visit if diagnosis is not determined.
Review pertinent investigations such as labs and radiology reports.
Code only procedures done during the visit as part of the clinic visit.
Determine sequencing of principal diagnosis and principal procedure.
The principal diagnosis may be a symptom or suspected condition not yet determined at the time of the visit.

diagnosis_rules.source_section: 1.4 Coding Conventions / 1.4.4 Outpatient and Ambulatory Patient Coding
rules: Use the highest available specificity in diagnosis coding.
Use symptoms/signs when a definitive diagnosis has not been established.
For outpatient visits, do not code probable/suspected/questionable/rule-out diagnoses; use the highest degree of certainty for that encounter.
Chronic diseases treated on an ongoing basis may be coded as many times as the patient receives treatment and care.
Code all documented coexisting conditions that affect patient care or management.
For outpatient diagnostic services only, sequence first the diagnosis/condition/problem chiefly responsible for the services; additional chronic conditions may be additional diagnoses.
For outpatient therapeutic services only, sequence first the reason for the encounter chiefly responsible for the services; use the service-specific V-code first where appropriate.
For outpatient surgery, code the reason for the surgery as the principal diagnosis.
For routine prenatal visits without complications, use the appropriate V-code principal diagnosis.

risk_areas: Undocumented services or services not compliant with documentation.
Un-billed services when comparing the record to what was submitted for payment.
Violation of HAAD official coding guidelines.
Documentation inadequate to support level of service billed.
Incorrect place of service or category of service.
Medical necessity not supported by available documentation.
top_checkpoints: Chief complaint or reason for visit missing or incorrect.
Assessment and/or plan not clearly documented.
Test ordered not documented on the patient encounter but billed.
Incorrect diagnosis or diagnosis not referenced correctly for medical necessity.

findings: Facilities should maintain written policies for clinical data reporting/submission.
Facilities should maintain healthcare documentation policies and employee orientation/training policies.
Facilities should maintain confidentiality, privacy, and security of PHI.
Facilities should maintain audit logs and synchronized clocks in EHR systems where applicable.
Facilities should avoid misuse and abuse of template documentation.
Facilities should use narrative diagnosis rather than dropdown-only diagnosis selection.
Facilities should train staff on their roles and responsibilities.
Provider communication with the auditor is vital.
Action based on chart-audit findings is a critical step in the audit process.
Charge entry accuracy and timing should be monitored.

coding_conflict_rule.source_section: HAAD Coding Manual / Clinical Coding Process Review
coding_conflict_rule.rule: If documentation, coding guidance, or audit interpretation conflicts, follow the locally applicable HAAD instruction first and do not mix guideline sets in the same audit interpretation.
coding_conflict_rule.note: Use this block for router precedence and for recommending coder-physician query when local rules are unclear.
